content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jeffrey Walter 
Sonoma City Attorney 
WALTER & PISTOLE 
670 W. Napa Street, Suite F 
Sonoma, CA 954 76 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-024 
Dear Mr. Walter: 
March 15, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
As City Attorney, may you draft documents necessary for the City of Sonoma to complete 
its monetary contribution to the Blue Wing Adobe Trust's purchase of Mission Bell Farm, given 
that two of your law firm's clients are co-chairing the Trust's fundraising effort? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, because the City's contribution to the Trust for the purchase of the Farm would not 
have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on any of your economic interests, including 
your clients as sources of income. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-024 
Page No. 2 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Sonoma ("City"), and you also own a law firm that 
represents private clients. 
A piece of property, commonly known as Mission Bell Farm ("the Farm"), is for sale within 
the City. A nonprofit corporation, known as the Blue Wing Adobe Trust ("Trust"), is attempting to 
raise sufficient money to purchase the Farm. The Sonoma City Council has agreed to pay a certain 
sum toward the purchase price of the Farm, on the condition that the Trust is able to obtain 
donations for t):ie balance of the purchase price. Should the full amount of the purchase price be 
obtained via donations, the City Council has directed you to draft documents imposing a 
conservation easement on a portion of the Farm, in exchange for the City's contribution, all subject 
to conditions satisfactory to the City Council. 
The members of the Trust Board have enlisted the support of various community members 
to assist the board in raising the funds and obtaining donations necessary to purchase the Mission 
Bell Farm. Two community members have been asked to co-chair the fundraising effort and make 
donations toward the purchase price. Those two community members are also clients of your firm 
and, therefore, sources of income. Other than co-chairing the fundraising effort, soliciting donations 
and possibly making donations themselves, your clients are not members of the Trust's Board, nor 
do they have any financial interest in the property, nor will they have any financial interest in the 
property should it be purchased. Sufficient funds have not yet been raised to purchase the property. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or using his 
or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. 
Interests 
Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the 
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official's interests. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise 
are defined in Section 87103, as pertinent to the facts provided potentially disqualifying interests 
include the following: 
• 
A public official's own finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate family. 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
• 
Any source of income aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or 
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. 
(Section 87103(a)-(e).) 


File No. A-19-024 
Page No. 3 
Here, you have economic interests in your own finances, your law firm ( as a business 
entity), and the two community member clients as sources of income, assuming they have paid you 
or your firm $500 or more during the year preceding any governmental decision implicating them. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
With respect to financial interests not expressly involved in a decision, a financial effect 
need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be 
recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably 
foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not 
subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on personal finances is material if the public 
official or the official's immediate family member will receive a measurable financial benefit or 
loss from the decision. (Regulation 18702.5(a).) 
For a governmental decision that will have a financial effect on a business entity not 
expressly involved in that decision, the materiality of the financial effect is analyzed under 
Regulation 18702.1 (b ). 2 That regulation states: "the financial effect is material if a prudent person 
with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial 
effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the 
value of a privately-held business entity." 
Further, Regulation 18702.3 provides that a foreseeable financial effect on an interest in a 
source of income, received by an official for services provided in the ordinary course of business, is 
material if the source is an individual that will be financially affected under the standards applied to 
an official in Regulation 18702.5, or the official knows or has reason to know that the individual 
has an interest in a business entity or real property that will be financially affected under the 
standards applied to a financii~J interest in Regulation 18702.1 or 1·8702.2, respectively. 
In this case, it is not reasonably foreseeable that any of your economic interests will be 
materially affected should you assist the city in drafting documents related to the Trust's purchase 
of the Farm. Governmental decisions related to the City's donation and the Trust's purchase of the 
Farm would not directly involve or implicate your personal finances or law firm. Although two 
clients of your law firm are associated with the Trust, their personal finances would not be affected 
by the governmental decisions at issue, as they have no economic interest in the Trust, the Farm, or 
the Trust's purchase of the Farm. Rather, the clients are volunteers who have simply donated money 
to the Trust. Accordingly, no disqualifying financial interest arises under the Act and you may take 
part in governmental decisions pertaining to the City's contribution and the Trust's purchase of the 
Farm. 
2 Regulation 18702.1 also governs the materiality of financial effects on a source of income where the source is 
a business entity. (Regulation 18702.3(a)( 4).) 


File No. A-19-024 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
~ 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

that two of your law firm's clients are co-chairing the Trust's fundraising effort?

CONCLUSION: Yes, because the City's contribution to the Trust for the purchase of the Farm would not
